coredata.dc:title: Constructing functionalized carbon quantum dots on amino-rich graphitic carbon nitride to enhance CO2 photocatalytic reduction: Critical role of functional group modulation
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Qian
Wang, Xinyu
Wang, Wenjun
Wang, Haiqiang
Chen, Qingguo
Chen, Jianmeng
Chen, Dongzhi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decoration have been widely acknowledged as promising strategy in photocatalysis, yet in-depth understanding of the effect of different functionalized CQDs on CO2 photocatalytic reduction is still lacked. Herein, three kinds of functionalized CQDs, i.e. carboxylic CQDs (CCQDs), amino CQDs (NCQDs) and sulfonated CQDs (SCQDs), were homogeneously anchored on amino-rich g-C3N4 (U1W1-CN) to investigate their CO2 reduction behaviors under simulated solar-light illumination. Structural analysis demonstrated that potential amide covalent bonds formed between U1W1-CN and CCQDs, and noncovalent conjugations might exist between U1W1-CN and SCQDs, while strong electrostatic attraction between U1W1-CN and NCQDs. Performance tests showed CQDs decoration greatly enhanced the CO2 reduction activity, with 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN exhibiting the optimal CO and CH4 productions of 40.94 μmol g−1 and 2.2 μmol g−1 under 4 h light irradiation, markedly higher than those of 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN. After comprehensive analysis, it was found that CCQDs decoration endowed U1W1-CN with better CO2 adsorption and activation. And due to the potential amide covalent bonds formed between CCQDs and U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN delivered the largest electron density and most abundant COOH– intermediates, contributing much to its superior CO2 reduction activity over 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN. It is expected that the critical role of functional group modulation on CQDs in enhancing CO2 photocatalytic reduction revealed in this work can provide valuable insights into the rational design of more advanced photocatalysts for targeted reactions.

N 1 s (a), C 1 s (b) high-resolution XPS spectra of as-prepared samples, atomic ratios of nitrogen and carbon species in CN and U1W1-CN (c), O 1 s (d) and S 2p (e) high-resolution XPS spectra of as-prepared samples.

In-situ DRIFT spectra of U1W1-CN (a), 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN (b), 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN (c), 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN (d) before and after light irradiation for 2 h when exposed to CO2 and H2O.
Schematic mechanism of CO2 photocatalytic reduction over three CQDs/U1W1-CN under simulated solar light irradiation.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) decoration have been widely acknowledged as promising strategy in photocatalysis, yet in-depth understanding of the effect of different functionalized CQDs on CO2 photocatalytic reduction is still lacked. Herein, three kinds of functionalized CQDs, i.e. carboxylic CQDs (CCQDs), amino CQDs (NCQDs) and sulfonated CQDs (SCQDs), were homogeneously anchored on amino-rich g-C3N4 (U1W1-CN) to investigate their CO2 reduction behaviors under simulated solar-light illumination. Structural analysis demonstrated that potential amide covalent bonds formed between U1W1-CN and CCQDs, and noncovalent conjugations might exist between U1W1-CN and SCQDs, while strong electrostatic attraction between U1W1-CN and NCQDs. Performance tests showed CQDs decoration greatly enhanced the CO2 reduction activity, with 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN exhibiting the optimal CO and CH4 productions of 40.94 μmol g−1 and 2.2 μmol g−1 under 4 h light irradiation, markedly higher than those of 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN. After comprehensive analysis, it was found that CCQDs decoration endowed U1W1-CN with better CO2 adsorption and activation. And due to the potential amide covalent bonds formed between CCQDs and U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN delivered the largest electron density and most abundant COOH– intermediates, contributing much to its superior CO2 reduction activity over 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN. It is expected that the critical role of functional group modulation on CQDs in enhancing CO2 photocatalytic reduction revealed in this work can provide valuable insights into the rational design of more advanced photocatalysts for targeted reactions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The heavy combustion of fossil fuels and the subsequent massive emissions of CO2 has aroused severe global energy crisis and climate change [1]. Converting the excess CO2 into high value-added chemicals can simultaneously alleviate the greenhouse effect and energy shortage, which has been demonstrated as an effective approach to achieve carbon neutrality and social sustainable development [2,3]. So far, a variety of CO2 utilization technologies, such as thermal catalysis, electrocatalysis, biotransformation etc., have been developed [4]. There into, photocatalytic reduction of CO2 with H2O, driven by solar light over the semiconductor materials, has been widely recognized as one of the most green and promising CO2 utilization strategies [5,6]. Despite tremendous efforts devoted and great progress made, efficient CO2 photocatalytic reduction is still thermodynamically and kinetically challenging, due to the highly stable structure of CO2 and the reaction process involving the transfer of multiple electron coupled with protons [7]. Therefore, the exploration of highly efficient photocatalysts has long been a top priority.

Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4, denoted as CN), a metal-free semiconductor with graphite-like layered packing structures and excellent response to visible light, has been widely applied in CO2 photocatalytic reduction [8,9]. However, CN obtained by traditional thermal polymerization usually possess a bulk structure with limited surface-active sites and severe charge recombination. Despite CN nanosheets have larger specific surface area and shorter carrier diffusion length, resulting in more abundant active sites, faster charge transfer, and the consequent better photocatalytic activity [10], the slow surface reaction dynamics still leads to the recombination of most photogenerated carriers migrating to the surface, thereby limiting the enhancement of CO2 reduction performance. Excitingly, introducing abundant amino groups to CN is effective in facilitating the adsorption and activation of CO2 [11]. Meanwhile, cocatalysts decoration can reduce charge recombination rates via trapping interface photogenerated carriers and lower the activation energy of reactants and overpotential of related reactions, being known as an fascinating strategy in accelerating the surface reaction rates of CO2 reduction [12]. To be mentioned, currently available cocatalysts mainly focus on precious metals, difficult to be applied in large scale due to the lack of reserves and high costs. Developing economical and efficient metal free cocatalysts is the key to promote the application of CO2 photocatalytic reduction technology.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are kind of typical metal-free cocatalysts featured with broad solar-light utilization and superior electronic conductivity, gaining extensive attention in photocatalytic applications. Note that, CO2 molecules can be preferentially adsorbed on the surface of CQDs through π-π interactions, conducive to the destabilization and activation of CO2, making CQDs an ideal co-catalyst for CO2 reduction [13]. Moreover, the abundant surface functional group of CQDs, such as carboxyl, sulfonic acid, amine, etc., gives rise to the excellent convenience for its anchoring on other substrates, as well as the easiness to enhance the optical properties through functionalization [14]. Feng et al constructed a nonpolar CQDs modified CN hybrid and discovered that the nonpolar CQDs simultaneously improved CO2 adsorption and photoinduced H2, thereby enhanced CO and comparable CH4 production in contrast to CN were obtained [15]. Hou et al. developed a amide-bonded CQDs-CN heterojunction photocatalyst, which presented much higher CO selectivity and stability than the noncovalent CN@CQDs [16]. All these revealed that the promoting effects and mechanisms involved are significantly influenced by the surface functional groups of CQDs. Despite some exciting results have been achieved in the CQDs/CN systems, whether the surface group type of CQDs has any effect on the physicochemical properties of CN is still unclear, and it is also a mystery which type of functionalized CQDs is most conducive to promoting the CO2 photocatalytic activity of CN. Therefore, it is an urgent need to understand the mechanism of different functionalized CQDs on CO2 photocatalytic reduction process, which may inspire the rational design and modulation of CQDs/CN system with high-efficiency in the future.

Herein, three different functionalized CQDs, i.e. carboxylic CQDs (CCQDs), amino CQDs (NCQDs) and sulfonated CQDs (SCQDs), were synthesized by a facile one-step pyrolysis method and subsequently uniformly anchored onto the surface of amino-rich g-C3N4. The intrinsic relationships between physicochemical properties and CO2 photocatalytic reduction performance among three functionalized CQDs/CN were investigated and compared. Furthermore, the underlying mechanism of different functionalized CQDs on promoting CO2 photocatalytic reduction activity, especially the distinct role of CCQDs, were comprehensively investigated. As far as we know, this is the first attempt to investigate the effect of different functionalized CQDs on amino-rich g-C3N4. It is expected the work conducted here can shed light on the rational modulation of highly efficient carbon based photocatalysts via surface functional groups modulation.

Urea and N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Poly (sodium 4-styrene sulfonate) (PSS, M.W. 70000), Citric acid (CA), branched polyethyleneimine (BPEI, M.W. 1800) were obtained from Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were of analytical pure and used without additional purification.

The synthesis of g-C3N4 substrates abundant with superficial amino groups were firstly prepared using the previous literature with some modifications [17]. Typically, 20 g of urea was completely dissolved in a 100 mL crucible containing 20 mL deionized water, which was then situated in a muffle furnace with a full cover and calcined at 550°C for 4 h. After cooling down, light yellow powder was obtained and labeled as U1W1-CN. Meanwhile, the compared bulk g-C3N4 was synthesized by directly heating to 550°C for 2 h using 20 g urea without deionized water and labeled as CN.

CCQDs, NCQDs and SCQDs were prepared via the simple low-temperature pyrolysis of CA and subsequent delicate dialysis treatment [18], with specific procedures detailed in Text S1. And the schematic illustration of the synthetic process of three CQDs was shown in Fig. 1 . The CCQDs decorated U1W1-CN composites were synthesized by a mixed roasting method. Specifically, 0.5 g of as-prepared U1W1-CN and a certain amount of CCQDs were codispersed in 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol and ultrasonically treated for 2 h. The solution was centrifuged and dried at 60°C, followed by the calcination at 200°C for 1 h. The obtained samples were labeled as xCCQDs/U1W1-CN (x = 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%), where x represents the mass ratio between CCQDs and U1W1-CN. The xNCQDs/U1W1-CN and xSCQDs/U1W1-CN composites were prepared using the same approach. Moreover, xCCQDs/CN, xNCQDs/CN and xSCQDs/CN were also prepared as a reference.

The physical structures were investigated by X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD, Rigaku MiniFlex 600, Japan, with Cu Kα radiation), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Shimadzu IR Affinity), and Raman spectra using a micro-Raman spectroscope (LabRAM HR Evolution, Horiba, Japan) with a 514 nm laser as the exciting source. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JEM-2100F) were used to study the morphological structures. Zeta potential was measured by a nano-zeta potential analyzer (Nano-ZS90). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out to study the surface chemical states, with the binding energy of C 1 s at 284.6 eV as the calibration. The specific area and pore structure were computed by the multipoint Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method with a low temperature static adsorption instrument (Autosorb-iQ, Quantachrome Instruments U.S.). UV–visible absorption spectra were obtained on a spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2600) equipped with an integrating sphere assembly, taking BaSO4 as the reference. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were acquired on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (FLS1000, Edinburgh Instruments, UK), using a 325 nm laser and a xenon lamp as the light source, while the Time-resolved PL decay spectra (TRPL) were measured using the same instrument excited at 514 nm. The transient photocurrent responses, electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and Mott-Schottky (MS) plots, were conducted using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660E, China) in a three-electrode system, where 0.2 M aqueous Na2SO4 solution was used as the electrolyte solution, and platinum electrode, saturated Ag/AgCl and the indium tin oxide (ITO) glass were employed as the counter electrode, reference electrode and the working electrodes, respectively. Temperature programed desorption (TPD) spectra were obtained over a PCA-1200 instrument (Beijing Builder Electronic Technology Co., LTD., China).

The CO2 photocatalytic reduction performance was evaluated in a batch reactor (PLS-SXE300, Perfectlight, China) equipped with a magnetic stirrer. Specifically, 30 mg of photocatalysts were uniformly coated on a glass slide (25 mm × 25 mm × 2 mm) with the aid of H2O, which was then placed on the top of a triangle bracket situated in the middle of the reactor. Then the reactor was tightly covered with a quartz glass, allowing the transmission of simulated solar-light emitted from a xenon lamp (PLS-SXE300UV, Perfectlight, China) equipped with an AM 1.5 G optical filter. Before the formal experiments, the reactor was purged by a pure CO2 flow at a rate of 50 mL min−1 to eliminate any gaseous impurities remaining in the system. Then the flow was modulated to a slow stream of 5 mL min−1 until the surface of the photocatalysts coating reached a saturation coverage with CO2. The reaction temperature was controlled at 30°C to eliminate the effects of the external environmental condition. Then, the light was turned on for 4 h, during which 1 mL of gas samples were periodically extracted from the reactor by syringes for tests. The CO2 concentrations were determined by a gas chromatograph (GC 9070II, Jiaxuan, Hangzhou) equipped with a packed column of JX.FFAP (Jiaxuan, Hangzhou, 0.32 mm × 30 m), a nickel conversion furnace and a flame ionization detector (FID). Blank experiments were also conducted in the same system following identical procedures under conditions without light, without H2O, without photocatalysts, and replacing CO2 with pure N2 (99.99%) to confirm the source of the products. Meanwhile, isotope (13CO2, 99 atom% C SIGMA-ALDRICH Co.) tracing experiments were also performed by gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS, Agilent, 7890A-5977B, USA) in the batch reactor to further explore the source of C in reduction products. Moreover, the selectivity of CO and CH4 was defined as the utilization efficiency of electrons for CO and CH4 formation in the total reacted electrons. As CO is a two-electron product and CH4 is an eight-electron product, the selectivity of CO (SCO ) and CH4 (

In-situ DRIFT measurements were performed in a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The samples were first placed in the reaction chamber and pretreated at 100°C for 1 h in a pure helium flow (30 mL min−1) to eliminate the impurity adsorbed on the surface of catalyst and in the chamber. The background spectrum was recorded after cooling to ambient temperature. Then CO2 and water vapor were purged into the reaction chamber for 1 h to achieve adequate adsorption, during which the absorption spectrum was recorded at intervals. The reaction chamber was then irradiated for 2 h and the FTIR spectra were collected for every 20 min.

The XRD patterns of as-prepared samples are displayed in Fig. 2 a. All the CN-containing samples revealed two characteristic diffraction peaks at 12.8° and 27.6°, which respectively attributed to the characteristic (100) and (002) facets of CN (JCPDS 87–1526) [19]. The (002) peak of U1W1-CN exhibited a positive shift compared with that of CN, indicating the gallery distance decrease of the basic layer units [17], probably due to the excellent exfoliation effect upon the addition of water. Besides, the XRD patterns of CCQDs and NCQDs revealed a broad peak at 22.6°, corresponding to an amorphous carbon [20], while that of SCQDs shifted to a lower degree, indicative of a larger lattice spacing. Nevertheless, due to the small decoration content and the homogeneous dispersion of CQDs [21], the diffraction peaks of three CQDs could hardly be observed in the CQDs/U1W1-CN composites. Similar findings were also obtained from the FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b). The skeleton structure of CN, including the characteristic breathing mode of triazine units (810 cm−1), stretching modes of CN heterocycles (1000–1700 cm−1), and the typical stretching vibration modes of N–H (3000–3420 cm−1) remained intact upon the decoration of different CQDs [22–24]. As for the three functionalized CQDs, besides from the typical absorption peak of –OH (3416 cm−1) functional groups, a serious of characteristic functional groups were also discerned. Specifically, C-OH peaks at 1220 cm−1 and –COOH groups at 1720 cm−1 were observed over CCQDs; Peaks at 1153, 1554, and 1651 cm−1, assigning to the stretching vibration of C-N and N–H of BPEI appeared over NCQDs; while the S-O stretching vibrations at 673 cm−1, the symmetric or asymmetric stretching vibrations of S=O at 1186 cm−1, and the stretching vibration modes of C=O at 1642 cm−1 emerged over SCQDs [18]. Such results robustly indicated the successful preparation of three different functionalized CQDs. Moreover, the N–H peaks of NCQDs at 1651 and 1554 cm−1 shifted to 1639 and 1540 cm−1, suggesting a strong interaction between NCQDs and U1W1-CN. However, the characteristic peaks of CCQDs and SCQDs were hardly observed overall 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, either due to their low loading content and equal dispersion of CQDs, in accordance with the XRD results.

Raman spectra were obtained to further investigate and compare the graphitization of three functionalized CQDs. In general, the D-band (1384 cm−1) and G-band (1574 cm−1) represent sp3 defects and sp2 hybridization of carbon materials, respectively, and their graphitization degree can be deduced from the ID/IG intensity ratio [25,26]. Literatures reports that the lower the ID/IG intensity ratio, the higher the carbonization degree of carbon materials, and the better their electrical conductivity will be [27,28]. The ID/IG ratios of SCQDs, CCQDs and NCQDs were calculated to be 0.58, 0.78 and 0.79, respectively, revealing that the carbonization degree of three functionalized CQDs came in the order of SCQDs > CCQDs ≈ NCQDs. Thus, it could be deduced that SCQDs might work best at facilitating the electron transfer.

Zeta potential was determined to explore the surface charges of CN, U1W1-CN, SCQDs, CCQDs and NCQDs. As shown in Fig. 2d, the additional introduction of carboxylic and multivalent −SO3 2− groups endowed CCQDs and SCQDs with more negative zeta charges. However, positive zeta potential value was observed for NCQDs due to the presence of abundant amino groups [18]. And by the same token, much less negative zeta potential value was observed over U1W1-CN (−13.76 mV) in comparison with CN (−17.19 mV). Besides, it can also be inferred that U1W1-CN and NCQDs could be assembled stably through electrostatic attraction, while U1W1-CN and CCQDs and SCQDs might be combined via other ways, which would be clarified in detail in the XPS analysis part.

As for the morphological structure of as-prepared samples, both SEM (Fig. S1a-b) and TEM (Fig. 3 a-b) images showed that U1W1-CN had a more ultrathin and porous structure than CN. SEM images showed three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites maintained the typical curled structure of U1W1-CN, and CQDs could hardly be observed probably due to the small size of CQDs (Fig. S1). SCQDs, while CCQDs and NCQDs demonstrated a rather uniform spherical shape with an average dimension of 2.51 nm, 2.32 nm, and 2.29 nm, respectively. Distinct lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.18 nm, 0.21 nm, and 0.22 nm could also be observed over three CQDs, corresponding to the (100) crystal planes of graphitic carbon [29]. Furthermore, a large number of CQDs (marked with yellow circles) could be distinctly distinguished over the TEM images of 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN composites, robustly confirming the successful decoration of CQDs on U1W1-CN.

XPS spectra were collected to investigate the surface chemical composition and states of CN upon decoration with three functionalized CQDs. The XPS survey spectra showed that characteristic peaks of N 1 s and S 2p emerged over NCQDs and SCQDs in addition to C 1 s and O 1 s peaks (Fig. S2), further implying the successful decoration of functional groups of BPEI or PSS on CQDs. The N 1 s spectra of all CN-containing samples could be deconvoluted into four peaks. The peaks located at 398.4, 400.2, 401.1 and 404.1 eV in CN and U1W1-CN could be assigned to the sp2 hybridized C=N-C, sp3 hybridized N-(C)3, −NHx, and the charging effect caused by п-п excitation, respectively [30]. After decorated with SCQDs and NCQDs, the C=N-C peaks exhibited a positive shift of 0.2 eV or 0.1 eV, probably due to the strong electron-withdrawing properties of −SO3 2− groups [31], as well as the electrostatic attraction between U1W1-CN and NCQDs. However, the C=N-C, N-(C)3 and −NHx peaks conversely shifted to lower binding energies upon the introduction of CCQDs, in spite of the electron-withdrawing effects of carboxyl groups, which might be related to the formation of amide bonds between U1W1-CN and CCQDs [32]. The high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectra of CN and U1W1-CN were almost identical (Fig. 4 b), where the peaks located at 284.6, 286.5 and 287.9 eV were assigned to the adventitious carbon, C-NHx and N-C=N, respectively [33]. Besides, several fresh peaks were observed after the decoration of three functionalized CQDs. Specifically, C-O peaks located at 286.4 eV emerged over all composites, and the O=C-OH groups at 288.7 eV as well as C-N peaks at 285.2 were clearly discerned over 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN, respectively. Moreover, the N-C=N peaks in 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN presented positive shift, while the N-C=N peak in 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN conversely shifted toward lower binding energies. To be noted, the peak shift happened to the C1s XPS spectra was consistent with the N 1 s XPS spectra (Fig. 4a). The atomic ratios of nitrogen and carbon species were summarized in Fig. 4c, the atomic ratios of −NHx and C-NHx over U1W1-CN were estimated to be 8.0% and 11.03%, much higher than those over CN (5.04% and 10.08%), suggesting that more amino groups were introduced over U1W1-CN.

As for the high-resolution O 1 s XPS spectra, two main peaks at 531.9 and 532.7 eV, respectively assigning to N-C-O and surface-adsorbed H2O, emerged over all CN-containing samples [34]. Three CQDs exhibited two typical peaks of C=O (530.9 eV/531.0 eV) and surface-adsorbed H2O (532.7 eV) functional groups. Besides, new peaks at 531.6 and 533.4 eV, representing C-O/C-OH and O=C-OH groups were observed over CCQDs; New XPS peaks at 531.6 and 533.6 eV, originating from C-O/C-OH and C-O groups appeared in NCQDs; And peaks at 532.1 eV and 536.0 eV, ascribing to the C-O-C/C-OH and S=O functional groups were observed over SCQDs. Besides, some characteristic peaks of CQDs were also observed over the composites. Specifically, the C=O (531.0 eV) peak belonging to SCQDs was observed over 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, the C=O (530.9 eV), C-O/C-OH (531.6 eV) and O=C-OH (533.4 eV) peaks originating from CCQDs appeared over 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN, and the C=O (530.9 eV), C-O/C-OH (531.6 eV), C-O (533.6 eV) peaks deriving from NCQDs were identified over 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN. All these new peaks robustly indicated that three functionalized CQDs were successfully loaded on U1W1-CN. The S 2p high resolution XPS spectra of SCQDs and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN were also analyzed. Two doublet peaks located at 167.3/168.9 eV and 168.1/169.4 eV were observed over SCQDs, which were attributed to the 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 orbits of the sulfur in SO2 − and SO3 − originating from the sulfonic acid groups of PSS, respectively [35]. Meanwhile, another two peaks at 163.3 and 164.5 eV, associated with 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of the sulfur in C-S-C [36], appeared over 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, revealing that the decoration of SCQDs on U1W1-CN were mainly achieved via the interactions between the S and C atoms.

The CO2 photocatalytic reduction activity of as-prepared samples was first evaluated. The main products detected over all photocatalysts were CO and CH4. Meanwhile, a series of control experiments with no light irradiation, no H2O, no photocatalyst, and using pure N2 gas substituting CO2 demonstrated negligible products, attesting that the products were originated from the CO2 feedstock than any other carbonaceous impurities. The results of 13C isotope tracing experiments further provided strong evidence for the carbon source of the produced CO and CH4 (Fig. S3). The influence of CQDs loading contents on the CO2 photocatalytic reduction performance of U1W1-CN had been investigated, and the results were illustrated in Fig. S4. The CO and CH4 productions of three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites exhibited a “volcano-like” dependence on the loading contents of CQDs. Lower or higher loading contents of CQDs would hamper the promotion of CO2 photocatalytic reduction activity, and 7 wt% was demonstrated to be the optimal loading content of three kinds of functionalized CQDs. This was probably because lower CQDs loading content was unfavorable to light absorption, while higher loading content would lead to the partial coverage of reactive sites of U1W1-CN and aggravate carrier recombination. The CO and CH4 productions of all CQDs/U1W1-CN composites were not only far superior than the bare CN and U1W1-CN, but also outperformed the corresponding CQDs/CN counterparts, revealing the pivotal role of CQDs cocatalysts and U1W1-CN substrates, as well as the dedicate interactions between functionalized CQDs and U1W1-CN in facilitating CO2 photocatalytic conversion. The performance of 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN, 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN were also investigated and compared (Fig. 5 a), with 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN presenting the optimal performance. The CO and CH4 productions of CCQDs/U1W1-CN achieved 40.94 and 2.20 μmol g−1, significantly higher than those of 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN (24.67 μmol g−1 and 1.06 μmol g−1) and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN were (22.90 μmol g−1 and 1.69 μmol g−1), which embodied the distinct role of surface functional groups over CQDs. Furthermore, Table S1 compares the CO2 photocatalytic reduction performance of CQDs/U1W1-CN with those reported in literatures, and despite the discrepancy of different reaction systems, it could be concluded that the samples developed in this study were pretty excellent, when taking the promotion effect of product yields in comparison with the pristine CN substrates. The product selectivity of as-prepared samples was also determined (see Fig. 5b). The CO and CH4 selectivity of CN were calculated to be 80% and 20%, and there were little differences in the product selectivity among different samples. To evaluate the regenerative performance of the CO2 photocatalytic performance over three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites, five consecutive recycle experiments were carried out, where the samples were subjected to thorough washing, centrifugal recovery and drying after every 4 h of light reaction, and then a new round of activity test was carried out. As presented in Fig. 5c and Fig. S5, the CO and CH4 yields of three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites were relatively stable in five consecutive runs, indicating that the reactive sites of CQDs/U1W1-CN could maintain intact after long-term reactions. Besides, the crystal structure and surface chemical states of the three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites after cycling tests were almost unchanged, demonstrating excellent structural stability (Fig. S6&S7).

CO2 adsorption and activation were of great significance to subsequent photocatalytic reduction reactions. The CO2 temperature-programmed desorption (TPD) curves were then firstly obtained. As shown in Fig. 6 a, two desorption peaks could be observed over CN and U1W1-CN in the temperature ranges of 90–120°C and 450–480°C, which were associated with the strong physiosorbed and chemisorbed CO2, respectively [37], and the desorption peaks in the high-temperature region were rather wide and flat. Stronger desorption peak intensity and lower desorption temperature were observed over U1W1-CN in comparison with CN, indicative of higher CO2 adsorption capacities, which was probably associated with its larger specific surface area (Fig. S8) and more abundant amino groups (Fig. 4c). Note that, apart from the peaks in the lower temperature region shifting toward higher temperatures, two fresh peaks emerged upon the decoration of CQDs with remarkably stronger intensities and varied peak centers. Specifically, peaks at 267°C, 340°C and 350°C in the CQDs/U1W1-CN composites were respectively ascribed to the π-π conjugation adsorption over CCQDs, NCQDs and SCQDs; While the peaks located at 436°C, 440°C and 454°C were probably attributed to the hydrogen-bond interaction between CO2 and -COOH groups [38], the strong electrostatic attraction between polar CO2 molecules and sulfonate group [39,40], and C-N covalent bonds between CO2 and marginal amino groups [41]. When comparing the peak intensity and positions of three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites, it could be deduced that 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN exhibited the highest CO2 adsorption content, followed by 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, and the CO2 desorption temperatures over 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN was much lower than the other two composites, revealing that CO2 molecules were more easily adsorbed and activated on the surface of 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN. Note that the surface area, pore size, and pore volume of three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites were much smaller than the pristine U1W1-CN (Fig. S8), it could be inferred that the enhanced CO2 adsorption capacity of CQDs-containing samples was probably not concerned with their morphological structures, but closely associated with the surface functionalized groups of CQDs.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra of as-prepared samples were obtained to investigate their light absorption properties. As shown in Fig. 7 a, the absorption edge of U1W1-CN was slightly blue-shifted compared with that of CN, indicative of its larger band gaps after amino functionalization treatment, which could be attributed to the well-known quantum confinement of nanosheets [42]. Nevertheless, the absorption edges of U1W1-CN displayed red-shift after decoration with three functionalized CQDs, revealing that CQDs could narrow the band gaps (Eg ) of U1W1-CN and broaden the light response range. It was reported that the strong chemical bond can be formed between CQDs and CN, resulting in the formation of sub-band energy level between CB and VB [43]. Besides, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra were transformed to obtain the Tauc curves, the Eg of as-prepared samples were estimated from the x intersection of the linear part of the curves, as displayed in the insert picture, using the Kubelka-Munk equation [44,45].

PL spectra were collected to investigate the separation and annihilation of photogenerated carriers (Fig. 7b). Due to the quantum confinement effect, U1W1-CN displayed slightly stronger PL intensity than CN. Nevertheless, the PL emissions of U1W1-CN were significantly reduced upon different CQDs decoration, indicative of the reduced charge recombination after CQDs decoration, which was because photogenerated electrons excited from U1W1-CN could quickly transfer to CQDs. 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN exhibited much lower PL intensity than 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN, suggesting that the decoration of CCQDs was more conducive to restrain the charge recombination than SCQDs and NCQDs. Moreover, the PL intensities of all CQDs/U1W1-CN composites were much lower than the corresponding CQDs/CN samples, revealing that it is of great significance to introduce surface amino to CN when decorating functionalized CQDs. Time-resolved PL decay spectra (Fig. 7c) were further employed to understand the lifetimes of photoexcited charge carriers. The TRPL attenuation data were fitted using a double exponential function (Equ.3&4) to acquire the average lifetime of charge carriers.

To further elucidate the separation of photogenerated carriers, EIS spectra (Fig. 7d) and photocurrent response (Fig. 7e) of as-prepared samples were acquired. Generally, the smaller the radius of the arc, the less the solid-state interface layer resistance [51]. Therefore, it could be deduced that the charge transfer resistance came in the order of 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN < 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN < 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN < U1W1-CN < CN, indicating that both the introduction of amino groups and three functionalized CQDs could accelerate the charge transfer of CN. Moreover, due to the enhanced light absorption (Fig. 7a) and reduced charge transfer resistance of 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN, its photocurrent responses turned out to be the highest, revealing that it possessed the largest electron density upon light illumination, which might be related with the potential amide covalent formed between U1W1-CN and CCQDs and contribute much to its outstanding photocatalytic CO2 reduction activity.

Band edge potentials of semiconductor materials are known to influence the thermodynamics and kinetics of photocatalytic reactions. MS curves and the XPS VB spectra of as-prepared samples were then obtained to observe their energy band structures. The slopes of the MS curves (Fig. 8 a) were positive, revealing that all samples were n-type semiconductor materials [52]. The flab-band potential (E fb, vs Ag/AgCl) of CN, U1W1-CN, 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN were estimated to be −0.89, −0.93, −1.06, −1.07 and −1.03 eV. As a general rule of thumb [53], the conduction band minimal positions (ECB ) of n-type semiconductors is ∼ 0.3 eV more negative than that of
E

, then the ECB of CN, U1W1-CN, 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN could be determined to be −0.99, −1.03, −1.16, −1.17 and −1.13 eV (vs NHE), respectively, using the equation of

. Compared with pure CN, the ECB of three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites were negative shifted, probably due to the synergistic effect of surface abundant amino groups introduction and CQDs decoration [17]. Since the CB positions were more negative than those of CO2/CO (−0.53 V vs NHE) and CO2/CH4 (−0.24 V vs NHE), which was sufficient to reduce CO2 into CO and CH4 in thermodynamics. The CB potential of 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN was the most negative among, indicating the photogenerated electrons produced over 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN possessed the strongest reduction ability, dynamically contributing to its highest CO and CH4 productions. Then, the VB maximum potentials (EVB ) of CN, U1W1-CN, 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN could be calculated to be 1.81, 1.78, 1.61, 1.58 and 1.55 eV (vs NHE, pH=6.8), based on the equation

, and the results matched well with the results of the VB XPS spectra (Fig. 8b) [54,55]. The energy band structures were schematically illustrated in Fig. 8c.

To understand the influence of different functionalized CQDs on CO2 photocatalytic reduction process of U1W1-CN, in-situ DRIFT spectra of as-prepared samples under dark and illumination conditions were collected (Fig. 9 ). After adequate adsorption with CO2 and H2O, monodentate carbonate (m-CO3 2−) peaks at 1040, 1100, 1354 and 1392 cm−1, bidentate carbonate (b-CO3 2−) peaks at 1309, 1580 and 1709 cm−1, bicarbonates (HCO3 –) peaks at 1467 and 1643 cm−1, emerged over CN [56–58], which were also observed over U1W1-CN and CQDs/U1W1-CN composites with different intensities and slight position shifts. However, the peaks at 1530 cm−1 only appeared in U1W1-CN-containing samples, which was probably ascribed to the adsorbed CO2 by the surface amino groups of U1W1-CN [59]. Besides, the b-CO3 2− peaks at 1560 cm−1 and carbonyl species at 1743 cm−1 were only observed in three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites, which might be related with the introduction of CQDs. Furthermore, two peaks of activated CO2 – at 1241 and 1667 cm−1, widely known to be a decisive reactive species in CO2 photocatalytic reduction process [12], were also observed; Thereinto, the peaks at 1241 cm−1 were only present in U1W1-CN and three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites with slight shifts, while the peak at 1667 cm−1 was only observed in 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN, which robustly embodied the function of surface amino groups of U1W1-CN and CCQDs decoration in facilitating CO2 activation, consistent with the CO2‐TPD results (Fig. 6).

Subsequently, in-situ DRIFT spectra of as‐prepared samples after photocatalytic reaction for 2 h were collected to explore the intermediates accumulated on the surface of samples (Fig. 10 ). Obviously, simulated solar light exposure quickly led to the vanish of the bands at 1468 (m-CO3 2−) and 1580 (b-CO3 2−) cm−1 over CN, along with the emergence of several HCO3 – peaks at 1479, 1573, 1591 and 1622 cm−1, as well as the m-CO3 2− peak at 1456 cm−1. And the intensity of the CH3O– band at 1160 cm−1, b-CO3 2− bands at 1309 cm−1, the m-CO3 2− band at 1044, 1097 and 1354 cm−1 over CN were significantly increased (Fig. S9) [12,58]. Similar changes also happened to U1W1-CN. Meanwhile, the intensities of the CO2 – peaks at 1238 cm−1, b-CO3 2− peaks at 1650 cm−1, HCO3 – peaks at 1477 cm−1 over U1W1-CN also became stronger, and the peaks initially at 1390 and 1700 cm−1 shifted to 1394 and 1704 cm−1, suggesting that some HCO3 – and b-CO3 2− species accepted photogenerated electrons but failed to be further reduced. Excitingly, light irradiation caused a more distinct difference on 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN. Peaks at 1241 (CO2 –), 1275 (b-CO3 2−) and 1686 (CO2 –) cm−1 shifted to higher wavelength of 1245, 1280 and 1694 cm−1. Concurrently, some fresh peaks appeared, including CH3O– (1160 cm−1), b-CO3 2− (1317 cm−1), and m-CO3 2− species (1428 and 1512 cm−1) [60]. And more importantly, in addition to the intensity increase of the m-CO3 2− peak at 1044, 1098, 1354 cm−1, HCO3 – species at 1394, and 1558 cm−1, the COOH– peak at 1588 and 1711 cm−1 [61], a crucial intermediate of CO2 photocatalytic reduction, became much stronger, indicative of the continuous occurrence of CO2 reduction. Nevertheless, all the COOH– peak over 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN (at 1712 cm−1) and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN (at 1558 and 1712 cm−1) oppositely became vanished upon light exposure. Such difference might be concerned with the interaction mode between CQDs and U1W1-CN, and the potential amide covalent bond between CCQDs and U1W1-CN might facilitate the production of COOH–. Subtle observation could find that carbonyl groups at 1743 cm−1 over three CQDs/U1W1-CN composites became weakened or even disappeared under irradiation. Besides, the m-CO3 2− peak at 1104 cm−1 and CHO– species at 1166 cm−1 came into sight, while the b-CO3 2− peak at 1312 cm−1 and m-CO3 2− peak at 1509 cm−1 got decayed over 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN [62]. Thus, it could be inferred that COOH– and carbonyl species were the predominant reactive intermediate, and the most abundant COOH– species produced over 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN might play a significant impact on its excellent CO2 reduction performance.

Based on the above investigations and analysis, plausible mechanism for the effect of three functionalized CQDs and amino groups decoration on enhancing CO2 photocatalytic reduction of CN could be proposed (Fig. 11 ). First, both the abundant amino groups on U1W1-CN and three functionalized CQDs decoration facilitated the adsorption and activation of CO2 molecules, with the adsorbed CO2 molecules over 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN being more abundant and reactive. Despite the bandgap of CN was slightly increased upon amino groups introduction, the further decoration of three CQDs effectively accelerated the charge transfer and separation of U1W1-CN upon simulated solar-light irradiation, thereby remarkably increasing the electron density of CQDs/U1W1-CN composites. And due to the potential amide covalent bonds formed between CCQDs and U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN delivered the largest electron density and the most abundant COOH– intermediates, thus achieving superior CO2 reduction activity over 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN.

In summary, CCQDs, NCQDs, and SCQDs were homogeneously anchored on U1W1-CN abundant with amino groups to investigate the effect of different functionalized CQDs on CO2 photocatalytic reduction. Structural analysis demonstrated that potential amide covalent bonds formed between U1W1-CN and CCQDs, while there might exist noncovalent conjugation effect between U1W1-CN and SCQDs, and strong electrostatic attraction between U1W1-CN and NCQDs. CQDs decoration significantly improved the CO and CH4 productions of U1W1-CN, with 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN exhibiting the optimum CO and CH4 productions of 40.94 and 2.2 μmol g−1 under 4-h simulated solar-light irradiation, remarkably higher than those of 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN, and it also demonstrated outstanding performance stability. Mechanisms analysis revealed that CCQDs decoration endowed U1W1-CN with more favorable CO2 adsorption and activation. And due to the potential amide covalent bonds formed between CCQDs and U1W1-CN, 7%CCQDs/U1W1-CN delivered the largest electron density and the most abundant COOH– intermediates, thus contributing to its superior CO2 reduction activity over 7%NCQDs/U1W1-CN and 7%SCQDs/U1W1-CN. It is expected that the critical role of different functionalized CQDs revealed in this work can provide valuable insights into the rational design of more advanced photocatalysts for efficient CO2 photocatalytic reduction.
